title: Public transport accessibility in villages in and around major Chinese cities
data_sources: Chinese National People's Government village records

data_scope.spatial_coverage: ten major Chinese cities and their surrounding villages (Beijing, Tianjin, Shanghai, Nanjing, Suzhou, Hangzhou, Wuhan, Guangzhou, Chengdu, Chongqing)
data_scope.key_metric: share/number of village population within a 10-minute walking-transit zone of a city-bound stop
data_scope.headline_finding: only 2 of 10 cities' villages meet SDG benchmark; lowest at 34% (vs official 80%+ claim)
data_scope.ring_design: 20 concentric rings per village for station-growth vs population-change matching
data_scope.exact_totals: aggregate stop/village/population counts not stated as a single headline number in available text

core_conclusion: Headline public-transport access gains hide deep rural shortfalls: against an official claim of over 80% convenient PT access in major Chinese cities, only two of ten cities' villages meet the benchmark (lowest 34%). New bus stations often fail to track population shifts, and the share of villagers on high-fare-only routes rose even as average fares fell. A narrow 10-minute walking metric masks added time and cost burdens, so SDG 11 needs a population-responsive planning approach.
why_it_matters: Audits an official SDG indicator against ground truth and overturns the 80%+ narrative; reframes accessibility beyond walking distance to include commute-time and fare burden; exposes a measurable planning-population mismatch; centres the under-studied peri-urban village.
why_nature: Policy-critical SDG question with global relevance; counterintuitive, headline-correcting finding backed by rigorous spatial data; links space, equity and well-being; reproducible open-data, multi-source design with Zenodo dataset and Code Ocean code.
added_value_to_my_research: Demand-weighted 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility surface using multi-component transit impedance
